item.title: Issue #2774 created
item.description:
Semantics of A / B ? C

PR #2747 introduced the syntax A / B ? C

I'm not convinced that the proposal was clear about the semantics, in particular the effect on duplicate elimination and sorting into document order. It might be worth pointing out that the new syntax also allows A / B ? C / D ? E, provided that the lookup ? C selects nodes.

Grammatically, A / B ? C parses as A / (B ? C), and it is subject to the rule that the result must be either all nodes, or all non-nodes; and if the result consists of nodes, then they are deduplicated and sorted into document order.

Now, the expression only makes sense if B selects either maps or JNodes. Let's look at an XNode example: consider

$docs/map-for-key("my-key", .)?"Jones"

Where map-for-key is an XSLT-defined function that returns a map providing an index by key over the nodes in a document. In this example, the expression map-for-key("my-key", .)?"Jones" selects nodes, so I think the expression as a whole is going to return those nodes deduplicated in document order.

More generally, consider $A // f(.) ? "key". If f(.) returns JNodes, while ? "key" returns atomic values, then the JNodes aren't going to be sorted or deduplicated, and the result will therefore be different from ($A // f(.)) ? "key" which will sort and deduplicate.

I suspect that evaluating this expression as ($A // f(.)) ? "key" would be more intuitive. But I'm not sure that we can achieve that, given the way that it parses. There are certainly cases where users will be quite surprised that an expression like A // B // C ? x includes duplicates in the result simply because the same C has been selected by more than one route.
